records.ety: [AS. staef a staff; akin to LG. & D. staf, OFries. stef, G. stab, Icel. stafr, Sw. staf, Dan. stav, Goth. stabs element, rudiment, Skr. sthāpay to cause to stand, to place. See Stand, and cf. Stab, Stave, n.]

defs: A long piece of wood; a stick; the long handle of an instrument or weapon; a pole or stick, used for many purposes; as, a surveyor's staff; the staff of a spear or pike.
quotes: And he put the staves into the rings on the sides of the altar to bear it withal.
With forks and staves the felon to pursue.

defs: A stick carried in the hand for support or defense by a person walking; hence, a support; that which props or upholds.
quotes: The boy was the very staff of my age.
He spoke of it [beer] in “The Earnest Cry,” and likewise in the “Scotch Drink,” as one of the staffs of life which had been struck from the poor man's hand.

defs: A pole, stick, or wand borne as an ensign of authority; a badge of office; as, a constable's staff.
quotes: Methought this staff, mine office badge in court, Was broke in twain.
All his officers brake their staves; but at their return new staves were delivered unto them.

defs: A pole upon which a flag is supported and displayed.

quotes: I ascended at one [ladder] of six hundred and thirty-nine staves.

defs: A series of verses so disposed that, when it is concluded, the same order begins again; a stanza; a stave.
quotes: Cowley found out that no kind of staff is proper for an heroic poem, as being all too lyrical.

defs: The five lines and the spaces on which music is written; -- formerly called stave.
An arbor, as of a wheel or a pinion of a watch.
The grooved director for the gorget, or knife, used in cutting for stone in the bladder.

An establishment of officers in various departments attached to an army, to a section of an army, or to the commander of an army. The general's staff consists of those officers about his person who are employed in carrying his commands into execution. See tat Major.

Hence: A body of assistants serving to carry into effect the plans of a superintendent or manager; sometimes used for the entire group of employees of an enterprise, excluding the top management; as, the staff of a newspaper.

records.ety: [G. staffiren to fill or fit out, adorn, fr. D. stoffeeren, OF. estoffer, F. toffer, fr. OF. estoffe stuff, F. toffe. See Stuff, n.]

defs: Plaster combined with fibrous and other materials so as to be suitable for sculpture in relief or in the round, or for forming flat plates or boards of considerable size which can be nailed to framework to make the exterior of a larger structure, forming joints which may afterward be repaired and concealed with fresh plaster.
